sections.paragraph:
The letter שׂ was used anciently without distinction, before the invention of diacritic marks, to designate both the simple sound of s and the thicker sound, which in German is expressed by fch, in English by sh. The same is still the case with s in the Irish language, as solâ, solace; and se (pron. she), he; si (pron. she), she. After these grammatical distinctions were introduced, the lighter sound was marked by a point on the left horn, the thicker by one on the right.

For the Hebrew שׂ the Chaldeans often, and the Syrians always (as not having the letter Sin), substituted ם, as שָׂבַר, סְבַר, ܣܒܰܪ; but the Arabs, by a peculiar property of their language, in almost all these roots have ش, as שָׂנֵא, شنأ; שָׂכַר, شكر; שָׂכַל, شكل, etc. (just as the people of Wirtemberg pronounce the German s with a stronger sibilant, ift, as if ifcht); the Arabs rarely retain س, as שָׂלָה, سلى. In the Hebrew language itself kindred letters are-(a) the other sibilants, as שׁ, ם, צ, ז, see page 575, A also, צָפַן, שָׂפַן, סָפַן; צָחַק, שָׂקַק ;שָׂחַק, זָקַק to strain; שָׂאַר and שָׁאַר to ferment.-(b) sometimes the aspirates, just like Greek ὗς, sus; ὕλη, silva; compare שָׂדָה i.q. הָדָה to spread out; סָלַךְ سلك i.q. הָלַךְ to go; שָׂרַג = אָרַג to intertwine, to weave; very often also in the formation of roots a sibilant is prefixed to a biliteral stock, as שָׂגַב i.e. גָּבַב to be high; שָׂגָא i.q. גָּאָה, etc.; compare γράφω, scribo; γλύφω, sculpo; τρίζω, strideo; tego, στέγω; fallo, σφάλλω, and many others.
